RESIDENT LOADER
Technical Specifications
We need to develop a resident loader that meets the following requirements:
- small size (ideally not more than 100k)
- work on bare Windows OS starting from Windows 2008/Windows Vista
which means either using built-in scripting languages (js, powershell v2.0) or writing in C/C++ is acceptable.
- working with unauthorized user rights, without attempts to elevate privileges and bypass UAC
"Resident" means that the bootloader is fixed on the machine and survives machine reboots, starts after the machine reboots.
It does NOT mean that the bootloader is constantly hanging in memory as a background process and waiting for something.
All it needs to do is survive a reboot.
The bootloader's anchoring feature can be disabled at the build stage, for specific environments and tasks.
Then the bootloader fixing can be taken by the bot itself (by command 18, see the corresponding protocol).
For this purpose, the loader must pass the path to its own file to the bot when it starts (via com.line or shared memory or somehow else).
Task of the loader:
- at the first startup on the machine, to fix itself with one of the fixing techniques (add itself to the schedule, autoloader, shortcuts, etc., see below)
- determine processor bitness, and self-update to the correct bitness version
- choose a link to download the main file according to the bit rate
- get the encrypted file from the server and decrypt it.
Encryption - XOR 1 byte, key - current UTC date in YYYYYYMMdd representation, written in hex.
e.g. 20200128 is the hex string 3230323030313238 (each digit is converted to 16-character representation).
It is important to get the time in UTC to use the correct date for machines in remote time zones.
- in case of communication or decoding errors, keep trying to receive and decode the load every hour.
- run the received file using fileless-techniques - process hollowing, process doppelganging, loading and running dlls from memory, or similar techniques,
WITHOUT SAVING TO DISK, EVEN IN INTERMEDIATE FORM.
- after running the file, terminate
UNDER NO CIRCUMSTANCES SHOULD A FILE DOWNLOADED FROM THE NETWORK BE WRITTEN TO DISK.
this is a fundamental condition.

If a scripting language is chosen for implementation, it should be taken into account that the implementation of fileless startup techniques (process hollowing/process doppelganging) is most likely very difficult to implement.
(process hollowing/process doppelganging) is most likely extremely difficult to implement.
Regarding formalizing the load as a .dll:
- this avoids starting an additional process, hence avoiding proactive detections.
In this case, the entry point to the .dll is the DllMain(DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH) function.
The loader should support working through proxy, if it is specified in the operating system settings (inetcpl.cpl -> Connection ->
Proxy settings).
Additionally, the loader should be able to:
- Resolve .bazar domains of the Emercoin blockchain and use them to find team servers
- update itself.
An additional link embedded in the code is used for updating.
You should only update if the version is different (arbitrary string)
(the server should give the file version to the HEAD /update
HTTP/1.1 request in the X-Tag header)
It is STRONGLY RECOMMENDED that you do not store your own body as an .exe file.
The usual way to provide persistence on the system is to run the .exe file via autoloader, scheduled tasks,
WMI services, etc.
However, it is preferable to make the following scheme:
- loader body is stored packed and encrypted in some container:
.txt file, certificate file, picture, long value in registry key, .cab file, etc.
- so the real body is invisible to antivirus.
- it is not an .exe that is launched, but a bootstrap script (.bat file) that unrolls and launches the bootstrap body.
and deleting the intermediate .exe
after execution.
- bootstrap script uses only utilities available in the bare OS
For example (very primitively and conventionally, just to demonstrate the technique), bootstrap.bat:
@echo off
REM get the bootstrap from the "certificate"
certutil -decode file.crt file.exe
REM run the bootstrap loader
file.exe
REM wait for the file to load and work.
REM delete the loader, since it has already had time to work and start the load.
del /f /y file.exe
The self-updating procedure should take this scheme into account and correctly load the new body into the obfuscated file.
If C/C++ language is used, you should prepare a CMake project to be able to rebuild the loader code with non-Microsoft compilers,
e.g. mingw, clang.
SERVER VALIDATION
Server validation is done by obtaining the current server date and verifying the digital signature of the date string by an asymmetric crypto-key stitched into the uploader.
This is a defense against SinkHole attacks by redirecting traffic from bots to a dummy server.
The HTTP header on ANY HTTP response must necessarily contain a date with time, for example:
Date: YYYYY-MM-dd HH:mm:ss
First variant
HTTP-response must contain Set-Cookie: header with cookie name SID and value in base64 encoding.
The cookie value is a digital signature of the DATE (WITHOUT time!).
* Second option
The digital signature is searched in ALL HTTP response headers.
If the signature matches the header values, the server is considered failed.
This change is made in order to improve traffic masking (false headers are included in the server response, which the client ignores,
and DPI systems are misled).
If the signature does not match, or the date (without time) does not match the local date, look for another server.
Server validation should be done on the first request to the server.
After validation, the server is marked as reliable and further validation is not required.
Validation should be repeated when resetting the connection and when changing the server address.
The validation code is attached.
CLIENT VALIDATION
To cut researchers off from the server and prevent merging files from new groups, server-side client validation is used.
In each HTTP request to the server, the Date field is included and the current date and time are set
A private key for asymmetric encryption is embedded in the lowader/bot.
The key changes from group to group
The locker/bot must sign the date value (after the substring "Date: " starting from the first significant character after a space), wrap the received value in base64,
and put it in any random header.
Cookies can be used, but not necessarily - you can also use headers prefixed with X- and any non-unique name (i.e. one that is already in common use).
4- The encryption algorithm is the same as for server signature verification.
DETERMINING C&C SERVER ADDRESS BY DOMAIN
The following strategies are used to discover the command and control server:
1. a list of "raw" IP addresses
2. hardcoded list of Emercoin domains that do not exist yet (backup)
3. domain name generation based on the current date (domain suffix .bazar is implied)
The algorithm in step 3 is designed so that the number of all possible domains is several thousand,
so there's enough to cover all registrations,
and at the same time the resolver's search time is not too long (of the order of a day or several days).
The algorithm is given in the appendix.
Search by Algorithm 3 should be done in packs of no more than 5000 domains at a time.
After that go to point 1.
After the first unsuccessful cycle 1..3, you should increase the intervals of server search up to half an hour between points.
When connecting to the server, it should be validated by digital signature (see above).
If the server is invalid, the search continues.
If a valid command server cannot be found in 3 consecutive attempts, either terminate or remove yourself from the system (determined by the build settings).
(determined by the build settings).
After resolving an IP address from the Emercoin domain, the IP address should be resolved by performing an XOR 254 operation for each octet.
For example, 124.245.101.251 (obtained from DNS response) -> 130.11.155.5
Since DNS information is available to everyone, this way we protect against abuses of DNS-recorded addresses.
The code of the ipxor.ps1 script in PowerShell:
$ip = read-host -prompt "Enter IP";
write-host $ip;
$newip = '';
($ip.split('.')
| foreach {
$octet = [byte] ( $_)
$octet = $octet -bxor 254;
$newip = -join($newip,'.',$octet);
write-host $newip;
BOOTLOADER PROTOCOL
HEAD / HTTP/1.1
Checking for updates
In the response we look for an X-Tag header containing an arbitrary string denoting the version of the loader on the server.
An update is performed if the version embedded in the loader does not match the version on the server.
GET / HTTP/1.1
Get the body of the load and run it.
Encryption is XOR 1 byte, key is the current UTC date in the YYYYYYMMdd representation, written in hex
e.g. 20200128 is the hex string 3230323030313238 (each digit is converted to 16-character representation).
POST / HTTP/1.1
Same as GET, plus sending system information.
The request body should be date-encrypted as described above.
THIS FUNCTION IS OPTIONAL.
INCLUSION IN THE BUILD BY CONDITIONAL COMPILATION!
The formatting of the response in the POST body corresponds to the same backdoor response.
If some information cannot be obtained without running external utilities, we don't send it!
this is an important condition.
The list of fields is shortened:
path=complete path to the backdoor binary file (if not using fully fileless technology)
os=3-7 digits containing major-version, minor-version and build of the operating system, if the system has them
(for example, for 6.1 build 7600 this would be 617600).
os[1]=sign of OS type (W=Windows) and OS version
os[2]=OS build
arch=architecture (bit): 86 or 64
cname=computer name
uname=user name
domain=name of domain or workgroup of computer obtained ONLY by calling WinAPI, e.g. NetWkstaGetInfo; no running external utilities!)
av[]=type of antivirus
ps=process list
